checks.a_analysis: The output faithfully represents the text found in the source document. The paragraph containing a long list of county abbreviations (HYDE, DARE, etc.) corresponds to the labels found within the map image in the source PDF. All headers and list items match the source content. No invented text or sections were added.
The source document does not contain explicit contact information like phone numbers or emails. No such information was fabricated in the output.

All sections of the source document are represented in the output, including the main title, date, the map (via an image element), the legend (Parcel Updates), the sources note, and the scale bar. The single-page document was processed entirely.
The source document does not contain any tables.

checks.a_analysis: The content within each section is complete. The list of quarters under 'Parcel Updates' perfectly matches the legend in the source. The 'Sources' section contains the full multi-line text from the bottom left of the PDF.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text extracted is accurate to the source. The long string of county names is a result of OCR on the map labels, which is a common and acceptable behavior for this pipeline. The primary textual information (titles, legend, sources) is preserved exactly.

checks.a_analysis: I have carefully compared the source PDF with the output JSON. There are no instances of text content being duplicated. The list of county names extracted as a paragraph reflects the text found within the map image and is not a duplication of another text block. All headers and list items appear exactly once in the output, consistent with the source document.

The heading hierarchy is logical and consistent. The document title is treated as the section title (H1 level). 'January 8, 2025' is correctly nested as an H2. 'Parcel Updates:' and 'Sources:' are assigned H3, with the sub-heading 'Most recent Quarter' correctly nested under 'Parcel Updates:' as an H4. No heading levels are skipped.
The source document does not contain any tables. The output correctly identifies no table elements.

The reading order follows the visual flow of the document from top to bottom. It begins with the title and date, followed by the map image and its text content (the county names), then the legend (Parcel Updates), and finally the sources and scale bar at the bottom. This order is logical and preserves the meaning of the original document.

The legend in the source document is a visual list of project statuses. The conversion successfully identifies this as a list and correctly captures all eight items in the same sequence as shown in the source legend.
There are no links in the source document, and the output correctly reflects this without any injected or fabricated links.

checks.a_analysis: The source document is primarily a spatial map of North Carolina counties. The HTML output completely reorganizes this information by removing the map layout and replacing it with a vertical stack of oversized logo fragments, a dense block of county abbreviations in plain text, and then the legend as a list. This shift from a visual/spatial organization to a linear text-based organization constitutes a major layout difference that changes how a user interacts with the information.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 1 shows a map of North Carolina where county names are placed within their geographical boundaries, serving as the primary visual organization.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML screenshot shows a list of county abbreviations ('HYDE DARE CART BEAU...') following oversized logos, with no map structure present.
checks.b_evidence_location: Main document body, starting after the title.

checks.a_analysis: The source document does not contain any tables. The legend and source information are provided as text blocks and lists, which are correctly identified as non-tabular content.

The most critical visual element of the source document—the map of North Carolina with its color-coded parcel status data—is entirely missing from the HTML output. Additionally, the north arrow graphic from the top left of the PDF is missing. While some logos are present, they are rendered as fragmented, oversized tiles that distort the original visual intent.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF features a central map of North Carolina and a north arrow in the top left corner.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML screenshot does not contain the map image or the north arrow image.

checks.a_analysis: There are no instances of content duplication in the HTML output. Each section of text and each logo fragment appears only once.
